82	class SerializedColumnInfo(MixinInfo):
83	    """
84	    Minimal info to allow SerializedColumn to be recognized as a mixin Column.
85	
86	    Used to help create a dict of columns in ColumnInfo for structured data.

89	    def _represent_as_dict(self):

94	class SerializedColumn(dict):
95	    """Subclass of dict used to serialize  mixin columns.
96	
97	    It is used in the representation to contain the name and possible
98	    other info for a mixin column or attribute (either primary data or an
99	    array-like attribute) that is serialized as a column in the table.
100	
101	    """
102	
103	    info = SerializedColumnInfo()
104	
105	    @property
106	    def shape(self):
107	        """Minimal shape implementation to allow use as a mixin column.
108	
109	        Returns the shape of the first item that has a shape at all,
110	        or ``()`` if none of the values has a shape attribute.
111	        """
112	        return next(
113	            (value.shape for value in self.values() if hasattr(value, "shape")), ()
114	        )
115	
116	    def __repr__(self):
117	        """Representation of SerializedColumn
118	
119	        Examples
120	        --------
121	        >>> from astropy.table.serialize import SerializedColumn
122	        >>> SerializedColumn({"a": 1})
123	        SerializedColumn({'a': 1})
124	        """
125	        return f"{self.__class__.__name__}({super().__repr__()})"

128	def _represent_mixin_as_column(col, name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=()):
129	    """Carry out processing needed to serialize ``col`` in an output table
130	    consisting purely of plain ``Column`` or ``MaskedColumn`` columns.  This
131	    relies on the object determine if any transformation is required and may
132	    depend on the ``serialize_method`` and ``serialize_context`` context
133	    variables.  For instance a ``MaskedColumn`` may be stored directly to
134	    FITS, but can also be serialized as separate data and mask columns.
135	
136	    This function builds up a list of plain columns in the ``new_cols`` arg (which
137	    is passed as a persistent list).  This includes both plain columns from the
138	    original table and plain columns that represent data from serialized columns
139	    (e.g. ``jd1`` and ``jd2`` arrays from a ``Time`` column).
140	
141	    For serialized columns the ``mixin_cols`` dict is updated with required
142	    attributes and information to subsequently reconstruct the table.
143	
144	    Table mixin columns are always serialized and get represented by one
145	    or more data columns.  In earlier versions of the code *only* mixin
146	    columns were serialized, hence the use within this code of "mixin"
147	    to imply serialization.  Starting with version 3.1, the non-mixin
148	    ``MaskedColumn`` can also be serialized.
149	    """
150	    obj_attrs = col.info._represent_as_dict()
151	
152	    # If serialization is not required (see function docstring above)
153	    # or explicitly specified as excluded, then treat as a normal column.
154	    if not obj_attrs or col.__class__ in exclude_classes:
155	        new_cols.append(col)
156	        return
157	
158	    # Subtlety here is handling mixin info attributes.  The basic list of such
159	    # attributes is: 'name', 'unit', 'dtype', 'format', 'description', 'meta'.
160	    # - name: handled directly [DON'T store]
161	    # - unit: DON'T store if this is a parent attribute
162	    # - dtype: captured in plain Column if relevant [DON'T store]
163	    # - format: possibly irrelevant but settable post-object creation [DO store]
164	    # - description: DO store
165	    # - meta: DO store
166	    info = {}
167	    for attr, nontrivial in (
168	        ("unit", lambda x: x is not None and x != ""),
169	        ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
170	        ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
171	        ("meta", lambda x: x),
172	    ):
173	        col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
174	        if nontrivial(col_attr):
175	            info[attr] = col_attr
176	
177	    # Find column attributes that have the same length as the column itself.
178	    # These will be stored in the table as new columns (aka "data attributes").
179	    # Examples include SkyCoord.ra (what is typically considered the data and is
180	    # always an array) and Skycoord.obs_time (which can be a scalar or an
181	    # array).
182	    data_attrs = [
183	        key
184	        for key, value in obj_attrs.items()
185	        if getattr(value, "shape", ())[:1] == col.shape[:1]
186	    ]
187	
188	    for data_attr in data_attrs:
189	        data = obj_attrs[data_attr]
190	
191	        # New column name combines the old name and attribute
192	        # (e.g. skycoord.ra, skycoord.dec).unless it is the primary data
193	        # attribute for the column (e.g. value for Quantity or data for
194	        # MaskedColumn).  For primary data, we attempt to store any info on
195	        # the format, etc., on the column, but not for ancillary data (e.g.,
196	        # no sense to use a float format for a mask).
197	        is_primary = data_attr == col.info._represent_as_dict_primary_data
198	        if is_primary:
199	            new_name = name
200	            new_info = info
201	        else:
202	            new_name = name + "." + data_attr
203	            new_info = {}
204	
205	        if not has_info_class(data, MixinInfo):
206	            col_cls = (
207	                MaskedColumn
208	                if (
209	                    hasattr(data, "mask")
210	                    and np.any(data.mask != np.zeros((), data.mask.dtype))
211	                )
212	                else Column
213	            )
214	            data = col_cls(data, name=new_name, **new_info)
215	            if is_primary:
216	                # Don't store info in the __serialized_columns__ dict for this column
217	                # since this is redundant with info stored on the new column.
218	                info = {}
219	
220	        # Recurse. If this is anything that needs further serialization (i.e.,
221	        # a Mixin column, a structured Column, a MaskedColumn for which mask is
222	        # stored, etc.), it will define obj_attrs[new_name]. Otherwise, it will
223	        # just add to new_cols and all we have to do is to link to the new name.
224	        _represent_mixin_as_column(data, new_name, new_cols, obj_attrs)
225	        obj_attrs[data_attr] = SerializedColumn(
226	            obj_attrs.pop(new_name, {"name": new_name})
227	        )
228	
229	    # Strip out from info any attributes defined by the parent,
230	    # and store whatever remains.
231	    for attr in col.info.attrs_from_parent:
232	        if attr in info:
233	            del info[attr]
234	    if info:
235	        obj_attrs["__info__"] = info
236	
237	    # Store the fully qualified class name
238	    if not isinstance(col, SerializedColumn):
239	        obj_attrs.setdefault("__class__", col.__module__ + "." + col.__class__.__name__)
240	
241	    mixin_cols[name] = obj_attrs
242

244	def represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl, exclude_classes=()):
245	    """Represent input Table ``tbl`` using only `~astropy.table.Column`
246	    or  `~astropy.table.MaskedColumn` objects.
247	
248	    This function represents any mixin columns like `~astropy.time.Time` in
249	    ``tbl`` to one or more plain ``~astropy.table.Column`` objects and returns
250	    a new Table.  A single mixin column may be split into multiple column
251	    components as needed for fully representing the column.  This includes the
252	    possibility of recursive splitting, as shown in the example below.  The
253	    new column names are formed as ``<column_name>.<component>``, e.g.
254	    ``sc.ra`` for a `~astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord` column named ``sc``.
255	
256	    In addition to splitting columns, this function updates the table ``meta``
257	    dictionary to include a dict named ``__serialized_columns__`` which provides
258	    additional information needed to construct the original mixin columns from
259	    the split columns.
260	
261	    This function is used by astropy I/O when writing tables to ECSV, FITS,
262	    HDF5 formats.
263	
264	    Note that if the table does not include any mixin columns then the original
265	    table is returned with no update to ``meta``.
266	
267	    Parameters
268	    ----------
269	    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table` or subclass
270	        Table to represent mixins as Columns
271	    exclude_classes : tuple of class
272	        Exclude any mixin columns which are instannces of any classes in the tuple
273	
274	    Returns
275	    -------
276	    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table`
277	        New Table with updated columns, or else the original input ``tbl``
278	
279	    Examples
280	    --------
281	    >>> from astropy.table import Table, represent_mixins_as_columns
282	    >>> from astropy.time import Time
283	    >>> from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
284	
285	    >>> x = [100.0, 200.0]
286	    >>> obstime = Time([1999.0, 2000.0], format='jyear')
287	    >>> sc = SkyCoord([1, 2], [3, 4], unit='deg', obstime=obstime)
288	    >>> tbl = Table([sc, x], names=['sc', 'x'])
289	    >>> represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl)
290	    <Table length=2>
291	     sc.ra   sc.dec sc.obstime.jd1 sc.obstime.jd2    x
292	      deg     deg
293	    float64 float64    float64        float64     float64
294	    ------- ------- -------------- -------------- -------
295	        1.0     3.0      2451180.0          -0.25   100.0
296	        2.0     4.0      2451545.0            0.0   200.0
297	
298	    """
299	    # Dict of metadata for serializing each column, keyed by column name.
300	    # Gets filled in place by _represent_mixin_as_column().
301	    mixin_cols = {}
302	
303	    # List of columns for the output table.  For plain Column objects
304	    # this will just be the original column object.
305	    new_cols = []
306	
307	    # Go through table columns and represent each column as one or more
308	    # plain Column objects (in new_cols) + metadata (in mixin_cols).
309	    for col in tbl.itercols():
310	        _represent_mixin_as_column(
311	            col, col.info.name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=exclude_classes
312	        )
313	
314	    # If no metadata was created then just return the original table.
315	    if mixin_cols:
316	        meta = deepcopy(tbl.meta)
317	        meta["__serialized_columns__"] = mixin_cols
318	        out = Table(new_cols, meta=meta, copy=False)
319	    else:
320	        out = tbl
321	
322	    for col in out.itercols():
323	        if not isinstance(col, Column) and col.__class__ not in exclude_classes:
324	            # This catches columns for which info has not been set up right and
325	            # therefore were not converted. See the corresponding test in
326	            # test_mixin.py for an example.
327	            raise TypeError(
328	                "failed to represent column "
329	                f"{col.info.name!r} ({col.__class__.__name__}) as one "
330	                "or more Column subclasses. This looks like a mixin class "
331	                "that does not have the correct _represent_as_dict() method "
332	                "in the class `info` attribute."
333	            )
334	
335	    return out
336

338	def _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info):
339	    # If this is a supported class then import the class and run
340	    # the _construct_from_col method.  Prevent accidentally running
341	    # untrusted code by only importing known astropy classes.
342	    cls_full_name = obj_attrs.pop("__class__", None)
343	    if cls_full_name is None:
344	        # We're dealing with a SerializedColumn holding columns, stored in
345	        # obj_attrs. For this case, info holds the name (and nothing else).
346	        mixin = SerializedColumn(obj_attrs)
347	        mixin.info.name = info["name"]
348	        return mixin
349	
350	    # We translate locally created skyoffset frames and treat all
351	    # built-in frames as known.
352	    if cls_full_name.startswith("abc.SkyOffset"):
353	        cls_full_name = "astropy.coordinates.SkyOffsetFrame"
354	    elif (
355	        cls_full_name not in __construct_mixin_classes
356	        and not cls_full_name.startswith("astropy.coordinates.builtin_frames")
357	    ):
358	        raise ValueError(f"unsupported class for construct {cls_full_name}")
359	
360	    mod_name, _, cls_name = cls_full_name.rpartition(".")
361	    module = import_module(mod_name)
362	    cls = getattr(module, cls_name)
363	    for attr, value in info.items():
364	        if attr in cls.info.attrs_from_parent:
365	            obj_attrs[attr] = value
366	    mixin = cls.info._construct_from_dict(obj_attrs)
367	    for attr, value in info.items():
368	        if attr not in obj_attrs:
369	            setattr(mixin.info, attr, value)
370	    return mixin

373	class _TableLite(OrderedDict):
374	    """
375	    Minimal table-like object for _construct_mixin_from_columns.  This allows
376	    manipulating the object like a Table but without the actual overhead
377	    for a full Table.
378	
379	    More pressing, there is an issue with constructing MaskedColumn, where the
380	    encoded Column components (data, mask) are turned into a MaskedColumn.
381	    When this happens in a real table then all other columns are immediately
382	    Masked and a warning is issued. This is not desirable.
383	    """
384	
385	    def add_column(self, col, index=0):
386	        colnames = self.colnames
387	        self[col.info.name] = col
388	        for ii, name in enumerate(colnames):
389	            if ii >= index:
390	                self.move_to_end(name)
391	
392	    @property
393	    def colnames(self):
394	        return list(self.keys())
395	
396	    def itercols(self):
397	        return self.values()
398

400	def _construct_mixin_from_columns(new_name, obj_attrs, out):
401	    data_attrs_map = {}
402	    for name, val in obj_attrs.items():
403	        if isinstance(val, SerializedColumn):
404	            # A SerializedColumn can just link to a serialized column using a name
405	            # (e.g., time.jd1), or itself be a mixin (e.g., coord.obstime).  Note
406	            # that in principle a mixin could have include a column called 'name',
407	            # hence we check whether the value is actually a string (see gh-13232).
408	            if "name" in val and isinstance(val["name"], str):
409	                data_attrs_map[val["name"]] = name
410	            else:
411	                out_name = f"{new_name}.{name}"
412	                _construct_mixin_from_columns(out_name, val, out)
413	                data_attrs_map[out_name] = name
414	
415	    for name in data_attrs_map.values():
416	        del obj_attrs[name]
417	
418	    # The order of data_attrs_map may not match the actual order, as it is set
419	    # by the yaml description.  So, sort names by position in the serialized table.
420	    # Keep the index of the first column, so we can insert the new one there later.
421	    names = sorted(data_attrs_map, key=out.colnames.index)
422	    idx = out.colnames.index(names[0])
423	
424	    # Name is the column name in the table (e.g. "coord.ra") and
425	    # data_attr is the object attribute name  (e.g. "ra").  A different
426	    # example would be a formatted time object that would have (e.g.)
427	    # "time_col" and "value", respectively.
428	    for name in names:
429	        obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]] = out[name]
430	        del out[name]
431	
432	    info = obj_attrs.pop("__info__", {})
433	    if len(names) == 1:
434	        # col is the first and only serialized column; in that case, use info
435	        # stored on the column. First step is to get that first column which
436	        # has been moved from `out` to `obj_attrs` above.
437	        col = obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]]
438	
439	        # Now copy the relevant attributes
440	        for attr, nontrivial in (
441	            ("unit", lambda x: x not in (None, "")),
442	            ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
443	            ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
444	            ("meta", lambda x: x),
445	        ):
446	            col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
447	            if nontrivial(col_attr):
448	                info[attr] = col_attr
449	
450	    info["name"] = new_name
451	    col = _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info)
452	    out.add_column(col, index=idx)
453

455	def _construct_mixins_from_columns(tbl):
456	    if "__serialized_columns__" not in tbl.meta:
457	        return tbl
458	
459	    meta = tbl.meta.copy()
460	    mixin_cols = meta.pop("__serialized_columns__")
461

class SerializedColumnInfo(MixinInfo):
    """
    Minimal info to allow SerializedColumn to be recognized as a mixin Column.

    Used to help create a dict of columns in ColumnInfo for structured data.
    """

    def _represent_as_dict(self):
        # SerializedColumn is already a `dict`, so we can return it directly.
        return self._parent


class SerializedColumn(dict):
    """Subclass of dict used to serialize  mixin columns.

    It is used in the representation to contain the name and possible
    other info for a mixin column or attribute (either primary data or an
    array-like attribute) that is serialized as a column in the table.

    """

    info = SerializedColumnInfo()

    @property
    def shape(self):
        """Minimal shape implementation to allow use as a mixin column.

        Returns the shape of the first item that has a shape at all,
        or ``()`` if none of the values has a shape attribute.
        """
        return next(
            (value.shape for value in self.values() if hasattr(value, "shape")), ()
        )

    def __repr__(self):
        """Representation of SerializedColumn

        Examples
        --------
        >>> from astropy.table.serialize import SerializedColumn
        >>> SerializedColumn({"a": 1})
        SerializedColumn({'a': 1})
        """
        return f"{self.__class__.__name__}({super().__repr__()})"


def _represent_mixin_as_column(col, name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=()):
    """Carry out processing needed to serialize ``col`` in an output table
    consisting purely of plain ``Column`` or ``MaskedColumn`` columns.  This
    relies on the object determine if any transformation is required and may
    depend on the ``serialize_method`` and ``serialize_context`` context
    variables.  For instance a ``MaskedColumn`` may be stored directly to
    FITS, but can also be serialized as separate data and mask columns.

    This function builds up a list of plain columns in the ``new_cols`` arg (which
    is passed as a persistent list).  This includes both plain columns from the
    original table and plain columns that represent data from serialized columns
    (e.g. ``jd1`` and ``jd2`` arrays from a ``Time`` column).

    For serialized columns the ``mixin_cols`` dict is updated with required
    attributes and information to subsequently reconstruct the table.

    Table mixin columns are always serialized and get represented by one
    or more data columns.  In earlier versions of the code *only* mixin
    columns were serialized, hence the use within this code of "mixin"
    to imply serialization.  Starting with version 3.1, the non-mixin
    ``MaskedColumn`` can also be serialized.
    """
    obj_attrs = col.info._represent_as_dict()

    # If serialization is not required (see function docstring above)
    # or explicitly specified as excluded, then treat as a normal column.
    if not obj_attrs or col.__class__ in exclude_classes:
        new_cols.append(col)
        return

    # Subtlety here is handling mixin info attributes.  The basic list of such
    # attributes is: 'name', 'unit', 'dtype', 'format', 'description', 'meta'.
    # - name: handled directly [DON'T store]
    # - unit: DON'T store if this is a parent attribute
    # - dtype: captured in plain Column if relevant [DON'T store]
    # - format: possibly irrelevant but settable post-object creation [DO store]
    # - description: DO store
    # - meta: DO store
    info = {}
    for attr, nontrivial in (
        ("unit", lambda x: x is not None and x != ""),
        ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
        ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
        ("meta", lambda x: x),
    ):
        col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
        if nontrivial(col_attr):
            info[attr] = col_attr

    # Find column attributes that have the same length as the column itself.
    # These will be stored in the table as new columns (aka "data attributes").
    # Examples include SkyCoord.ra (what is typically considered the data and is
    # always an array) and Skycoord.obs_time (which can be a scalar or an
    # array).
    data_attrs = [
        key
        for key, value in obj_attrs.items()
        if getattr(value, "shape", ())[:1] == col.shape[:1]
    ]

    for data_attr in data_attrs:
        data = obj_attrs[data_attr]

        # New column name combines the old name and attribute
        # (e.g. skycoord.ra, skycoord.dec).unless it is the primary data
        # attribute for the column (e.g. value for Quantity or data for
        # MaskedColumn).  For primary data, we attempt to store any info on
        # the format, etc., on the column, but not for ancillary data (e.g.,
        # no sense to use a float format for a mask).
        is_primary = data_attr == col.info._represent_as_dict_primary_data
        if is_primary:
            new_name = name
            new_info = info
        else:
            new_name = name + "." + data_attr
            new_info = {}

        if not has_info_class(data, MixinInfo):
            col_cls = (
                MaskedColumn
                if (
                    hasattr(data, "mask")
                    and np.any(data.mask != np.zeros((), data.mask.dtype))
                )
                else Column
            )
            data = col_cls(data, name=new_name, **new_info)
            if is_primary:
                # Don't store info in the __serialized_columns__ dict for this column
                # since this is redundant with info stored on the new column.
                info = {}

        # Recurse. If this is anything that needs further serialization (i.e.,
        # a Mixin column, a structured Column, a MaskedColumn for which mask is
        # stored, etc.), it will define obj_attrs[new_name]. Otherwise, it will
        # just add to new_cols and all we have to do is to link to the new name.
        _represent_mixin_as_column(data, new_name, new_cols, obj_attrs)
        obj_attrs[data_attr] = SerializedColumn(
            obj_attrs.pop(new_name, {"name": new_name})
        )

    # Strip out from info any attributes defined by the parent,
    # and store whatever remains.
    for attr in col.info.attrs_from_parent:
        if attr in info:
            del info[attr]
    if info:
        obj_attrs["__info__"] = info

    # Store the fully qualified class name
    if not isinstance(col, SerializedColumn):
        obj_attrs.setdefault("__class__", col.__module__ + "." + col.__class__.__name__)

    mixin_cols[name] = obj_attrs


def represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl, exclude_classes=()):
    """Represent input Table ``tbl`` using only `~astropy.table.Column`
    or  `~astropy.table.MaskedColumn` objects.

    This function represents any mixin columns like `~astropy.time.Time` in
    ``tbl`` to one or more plain ``~astropy.table.Column`` objects and returns
    a new Table.  A single mixin column may be split into multiple column
    components as needed for fully representing the column.  This includes the
    possibility of recursive splitting, as shown in the example below.  The
    new column names are formed as ``<column_name>.<component>``, e.g.
    ``sc.ra`` for a `~astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord` column named ``sc``.

    In addition to splitting columns, this function updates the table ``meta``
    dictionary to include a dict named ``__serialized_columns__`` which provides
    additional information needed to construct the original mixin columns from
    the split columns.

    This function is used by astropy I/O when writing tables to ECSV, FITS,
    HDF5 formats.

    Note that if the table does not include any mixin columns then the original
    table is returned with no update to ``meta``.

    Parameters
    ----------
    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table` or subclass
        Table to represent mixins as Columns
    exclude_classes : tuple of class
        Exclude any mixin columns which are instannces of any classes in the tuple

    Returns
    -------
    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table`
        New Table with updated columns, or else the original input ``tbl``

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from astropy.table import Table, represent_mixins_as_columns
    >>> from astropy.time import Time
    >>> from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord

    >>> x = [100.0, 200.0]
    >>> obstime = Time([1999.0, 2000.0], format='jyear')
    >>> sc = SkyCoord([1, 2], [3, 4], unit='deg', obstime=obstime)
    >>> tbl = Table([sc, x], names=['sc', 'x'])
    >>> represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl)
    <Table length=2>
     sc.ra   sc.dec sc.obstime.jd1 sc.obstime.jd2    x
      deg     deg
    float64 float64    float64        float64     float64
    ------- ------- -------------- -------------- -------
        1.0     3.0      2451180.0          -0.25   100.0
        2.0     4.0      2451545.0            0.0   200.0

    """
    # Dict of metadata for serializing each column, keyed by column name.
    # Gets filled in place by _represent_mixin_as_column().
    mixin_cols = {}

    # List of columns for the output table.  For plain Column objects
    # this will just be the original column object.
    new_cols = []

    # Go through table columns and represent each column as one or more
    # plain Column objects (in new_cols) + metadata (in mixin_cols).
    for col in tbl.itercols():
        _represent_mixin_as_column(
            col, col.info.name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=exclude_classes
        )

    # If no metadata was created then just return the original table.
    if mixin_cols:
        meta = deepcopy(tbl.meta)
        meta["__serialized_columns__"] = mixin_cols
        out = Table(new_cols, meta=meta, copy=False)
    else:
        out = tbl

    for col in out.itercols():
        if not isinstance(col, Column) and col.__class__ not in exclude_classes:
            # This catches columns for which info has not been set up right and
            # therefore were not converted. See the corresponding test in
            # test_mixin.py for an example.
            raise TypeError(
                "failed to represent column "
                f"{col.info.name!r} ({col.__class__.__name__}) as one "
                "or more Column subclasses. This looks like a mixin class "
                "that does not have the correct _represent_as_dict() method "
                "in the class `info` attribute."
            )

    return out


def _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info):
    # If this is a supported class then import the class and run
    # the _construct_from_col method.  Prevent accidentally running
    # untrusted code by only importing known astropy classes.
    cls_full_name = obj_attrs.pop("__class__", None)
    if cls_full_name is None:
        # We're dealing with a SerializedColumn holding columns, stored in
        # obj_attrs. For this case, info holds the name (and nothing else).
        mixin = SerializedColumn(obj_attrs)
        mixin.info.name = info["name"]
        return mixin

    # We translate locally created skyoffset frames and treat all
    # built-in frames as known.
    if cls_full_name.startswith("abc.SkyOffset"):
        cls_full_name = "astropy.coordinates.SkyOffsetFrame"
    elif (
        cls_full_name not in __construct_mixin_classes
        and not cls_full_name.startswith("astropy.coordinates.builtin_frames")
    ):
        raise ValueError(f"unsupported class for construct {cls_full_name}")

    mod_name, _, cls_name = cls_full_name.rpartition(".")
    module = import_module(mod_name)
    cls = getattr(module, cls_name)
    for attr, value in info.items():
        if attr in cls.info.attrs_from_parent:
            obj_attrs[attr] = value
    mixin = cls.info._construct_from_dict(obj_attrs)
    for attr, value in info.items():
        if attr not in obj_attrs:
            setattr(mixin.info, attr, value)
    return mixin


class _TableLite(OrderedDict):
    """
    Minimal table-like object for _construct_mixin_from_columns.  This allows
    manipulating the object like a Table but without the actual overhead
    for a full Table.

    More pressing, there is an issue with constructing MaskedColumn, where the
    encoded Column components (data, mask) are turned into a MaskedColumn.
    When this happens in a real table then all other columns are immediately
    Masked and a warning is issued. This is not desirable.
    """

    def add_column(self, col, index=0):
        colnames = self.colnames
        self[col.info.name] = col
        for ii, name in enumerate(colnames):
            if ii >= index:
                self.move_to_end(name)

    @property
    def colnames(self):
        return list(self.keys())

    def itercols(self):
        return self.values()


def _construct_mixin_from_columns(new_name, obj_attrs, out):
    data_attrs_map = {}
    for name, val in obj_attrs.items():
        if isinstance(val, SerializedColumn):
            # A SerializedColumn can just link to a serialized column using a name
            # (e.g., time.jd1), or itself be a mixin (e.g., coord.obstime).  Note
            # that in principle a mixin could have include a column called 'name',
            # hence we check whether the value is actually a string (see gh-13232).
            if "name" in val and isinstance(val["name"], str):
                data_attrs_map[val["name"]] = name
            else:
                out_name = f"{new_name}.{name}"
                _construct_mixin_from_columns(out_name, val, out)
                data_attrs_map[out_name] = name

    for name in data_attrs_map.values():
        del obj_attrs[name]

    # The order of data_attrs_map may not match the actual order, as it is set
    # by the yaml description.  So, sort names by position in the serialized table.
    # Keep the index of the first column, so we can insert the new one there later.
    names = sorted(data_attrs_map, key=out.colnames.index)
    idx = out.colnames.index(names[0])

    # Name is the column name in the table (e.g. "coord.ra") and
    # data_attr is the object attribute name  (e.g. "ra").  A different
    # example would be a formatted time object that would have (e.g.)
    # "time_col" and "value", respectively.
    for name in names:
        obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]] = out[name]
        del out[name]

    info = obj_attrs.pop("__info__", {})
    if len(names) == 1:
        # col is the first and only serialized column; in that case, use info
        # stored on the column. First step is to get that first column which
        # has been moved from `out` to `obj_attrs` above.
        col = obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]]

        # Now copy the relevant attributes
        for attr, nontrivial in (
            ("unit", lambda x: x not in (None, "")),
            ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
            ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
            ("meta", lambda x: x),
        ):
            col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
            if nontrivial(col_attr):
                info[attr] = col_attr

    info["name"] = new_name
    col = _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info)
    out.add_column(col, index=idx)


def _construct_mixins_from_columns(tbl):
    if "__serialized_columns__" not in tbl.meta:
        return tbl

    meta = tbl.meta.copy()
    mixin_cols = meta.pop("__serialized_columns__")

    out = _TableLite(tbl.columns)

    for new_name, obj_attrs in mixin_cols.items():
        _construct_mixin_from_columns(new_name, obj_attrs, out)

    # If no quantity subclasses are in the output then output as Table.
    # For instance ascii.read(file, format='ecsv') doesn't specify an
    # output class and should return the minimal table class that
    # represents the table file.
    has_quantities = any(isinstance(col.info, QuantityInfo) for col in out.itercols())
    out_cls = QTable if has_quantities else Table

    return out_cls(list(out.values()), names=out.colnames, copy=False, meta=meta)

95	    """Subclass of dict used to serialize  mixin columns.

99	    array-like attribute) that is serialized as a column in the table.

101	    """

108

128	def _represent_mixin_as_column(col, name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=()):
129	    """Carry out processing needed to serialize ``col`` in an output table
130	    consisting purely of plain ``Column`` or ``MaskedColumn`` columns.  This
131	    relies on the object determine if any transformation is required and may
132	    depend on the ``serialize_method`` and ``serialize_context`` context
133	    variables.  For instance a ``MaskedColumn`` may be stored directly to
134	    FITS, but can also be serialized as separate data and mask columns.
135	
136	    This function builds up a list of plain columns in the ``new_cols`` arg (which
137	    is passed as a persistent list).  This includes both plain columns from the
138	    original table and plain columns that represent data from serialized columns
139	    (e.g. ``jd1`` and ``jd2`` arrays from a ``Time`` column).
140	
141	    For serialized columns the ``mixin_cols`` dict is updated with required
142	    attributes and information to subsequently reconstruct the table.
143	
144	    Table mixin columns are always serialized and get represented by one
145	    or more data columns.  In earlier versions of the code *only* mixin
146	    columns were serialized, hence the use within this code of "mixin"
147	    to imply serialization.  Starting with version 3.1, the non-mixin
148	    ``MaskedColumn`` can also be serialized.
149	    """
150	    obj_attrs = col.info._represent_as_dict()
151	
152	    # If serialization is not required (see function docstring above)
153	    # or explicitly specified as excluded, then treat as a normal column.
154	    if not obj_attrs or col.__class__ in exclude_classes:
155	        new_cols.append(col)
156	        return
157	
158	    # Subtlety here is handling mixin info attributes.  The basic list of such
159	    # attributes is: 'name', 'unit', 'dtype', 'format', 'description', 'meta'.
160	    # - name: handled directly [DON'T store]
161	    # - unit: DON'T store if this is a parent attribute
162	    # - dtype: captured in plain Column if relevant [DON'T store]
163	    # - format: possibly irrelevant but settable post-object creation [DO store]
164	    # - description: DO store
165	    # - meta: DO store
166	    info = {}
167	    for attr, nontrivial in (
168	        ("unit", lambda x: x is not None and x != ""),
169	        ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
170	        ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
171	        ("meta", lambda x: x),
172	    ):
173	        col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
174	        if nontrivial(col_attr):
175	            info[attr] = col_attr
176	
177	    # Find column attributes that have the same length as the column itself.
178	    # These will be stored in the table as new columns (aka "data attributes").
179	    # Examples include SkyCoord.ra (what is typically considered the data and is
180	    # always an array) and Skycoord.obs_time (which can be a scalar or an
181	    # array).
182	    data_attrs = [
183	        key
184	        for key, value in obj_attrs.items()
185	        if getattr(value, "shape", ())[:1] == col.shape[:1]
186	    ]
187	
188	    for data_attr in data_attrs:
189	        data = obj_attrs[data_attr]
190	
191	        # New column name combines the old name and attribute
192	        # (e.g. skycoord.ra, skycoord.dec).unless it is the primary data
193	        # attribute for the column (e.g. value for Quantity or data for
194	        # MaskedColumn).  For primary data, we attempt to store any info on
195	        # the format, etc., on the column, but not for ancillary data (e.g.,
196	        # no sense to use a float format for a mask).
197	        is_primary = data_attr == col.info._represent_as_dict_primary_data
198	        if is_primary:
199	            new_name = name
200	            new_info = info
201	        else:
202	            new_name = name + "." + data_attr
203	            new_info = {}
204	
205	        if not has_info_class(data, MixinInfo):
206	            col_cls = (
207	                MaskedColumn
208	                if (
209	                    hasattr(data, "mask")
210	                    and np.any(data.mask != np.zeros((), data.mask.dtype))
211	                )
212	                else Column
213	            )
214	            data = col_cls(data, name=new_name, **new_info)
215	            if is_primary:
216	                # Don't store info in the __serialized_columns__ dict for this column
217	                # since this is redundant with info stored on the new column.
218	                info = {}
219	
220	        # Recurse. If this is anything that needs further serialization (i.e.,
221	        # a Mixin column, a structured Column, a MaskedColumn for which mask is
222	        # stored, etc.), it will define obj_attrs[new_name]. Otherwise, it will
223	        # just add to new_cols and all we have to do is to link to the new name.
224	        _represent_mixin_as_column(data, new_name, new_cols, obj_attrs)
225	        obj_attrs[data_attr] = SerializedColumn(
226	            obj_attrs.pop(new_name, {"name": new_name})
227	        )
228	
229	    # Strip out from info any attributes defined by the parent,
230	    # and store whatever remains.
231	    for attr in col.info.attrs_from_parent:
232	        if attr in info:
233	            del info[attr]
234	    if info:
235	        obj_attrs["__info__"] = info
236	
237	    # Store the fully qualified class name
238	    if not isinstance(col, SerializedColumn):
239	        obj_attrs.setdefault("__class__", col.__module__ + "." + col.__class__.__name__)
240	
241	    mixin_cols[name] = obj_attrs

244	def represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl, exclude_classes=()):
245	    """Represent input Table ``tbl`` using only `~astropy.table.Column`
246	    or  `~astropy.table.MaskedColumn` objects.
247	
248	    This function represents any mixin columns like `~astropy.time.Time` in
249	    ``tbl`` to one or more plain ``~astropy.table.Column`` objects and returns
250	    a new Table.  A single mixin column may be split into multiple column
251	    components as needed for fully representing the column.  This includes the
252	    possibility of recursive splitting, as shown in the example below.  The
253	    new column names are formed as ``<column_name>.<component>``, e.g.
254	    ``sc.ra`` for a `~astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord` column named ``sc``.
255	
256	    In addition to splitting columns, this function updates the table ``meta``
257	    dictionary to include a dict named ``__serialized_columns__`` which provides
258	    additional information needed to construct the original mixin columns from
259	    the split columns.
260	
261	    This function is used by astropy I/O when writing tables to ECSV, FITS,
262	    HDF5 formats.
263	
264	    Note that if the table does not include any mixin columns then the original
265	    table is returned with no update to ``meta``.
266	
267	    Parameters
268	    ----------
269	    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table` or subclass
270	        Table to represent mixins as Columns
271	    exclude_classes : tuple of class
272	        Exclude any mixin columns which are instannces of any classes in the tuple
273	
274	    Returns
275	    -------
276	    tbl : `~astropy.table.Table`
277	        New Table with updated columns, or else the original input ``tbl``
278	
279	    Examples
280	    --------
281	    >>> from astropy.table import Table, represent_mixins_as_columns
282	    >>> from astropy.time import Time
283	    >>> from astropy.coordinates import SkyCoord
284	
285	    >>> x = [100.0, 200.0]
286	    >>> obstime = Time([1999.0, 2000.0], format='jyear')
287	    >>> sc = SkyCoord([1, 2], [3, 4], unit='deg', obstime=obstime)
288	    >>> tbl = Table([sc, x], names=['sc', 'x'])
289	    >>> represent_mixins_as_columns(tbl)
290	    <Table length=2>
291	     sc.ra   sc.dec sc.obstime.jd1 sc.obstime.jd2    x
292	      deg     deg
293	    float64 float64    float64        float64     float64
294	    ------- ------- -------------- -------------- -------
295	        1.0     3.0      2451180.0          -0.25   100.0
296	        2.0     4.0      2451545.0            0.0   200.0
297	
298	    """
299	    # Dict of metadata for serializing each column, keyed by column name.
300	    # Gets filled in place by _represent_mixin_as_column().
301	    mixin_cols = {}
302	
303	    # List of columns for the output table.  For plain Column objects
304	    # this will just be the original column object.
305	    new_cols = []
306	
307	    # Go through table columns and represent each column as one or more
308	    # plain Column objects (in new_cols) + metadata (in mixin_cols).
309	    for col in tbl.itercols():
310	        _represent_mixin_as_column(
311	            col, col.info.name, new_cols, mixin_cols, exclude_classes=exclude_classes
312	        )
313	
314	    # If no metadata was created then just return the original table.
315	    if mixin_cols:
316	        meta = deepcopy(tbl.meta)
317	        meta["__serialized_columns__"] = mixin_cols
318	        out = Table(new_cols, meta=meta, copy=False)
319	    else:
320	        out = tbl
321	
322	    for col in out.itercols():
323	        if not isinstance(col, Column) and col.__class__ not in exclude_classes:
324	            # This catches columns for which info has not been set up right and
325	            # therefore were not converted. See the corresponding test in
326	            # test_mixin.py for an example.
327	            raise TypeError(
328	                "failed to represent column "
329	                f"{col.info.name!r} ({col.__class__.__name__}) as one "
330	                "or more Column subclasses. This looks like a mixin class "
331	                "that does not have the correct _represent_as_dict() method "
332	                "in the class `info` attribute."
333	            )
334	
335	    return out

338	def _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info):
339	    # If this is a supported class then import the class and run
340	    # the _construct_from_col method.  Prevent accidentally running
341	    # untrusted code by only importing known astropy classes.
342	    cls_full_name = obj_attrs.pop("__class__", None)
343	    if cls_full_name is None:
344	        # We're dealing with a SerializedColumn holding columns, stored in
345	        # obj_attrs. For this case, info holds the name (and nothing else).
346	        mixin = SerializedColumn(obj_attrs)
347	        mixin.info.name = info["name"]
348	        return mixin
349	
350	    # We translate locally created skyoffset frames and treat all
351	    # built-in frames as known.
352	    if cls_full_name.startswith("abc.SkyOffset"):
353	        cls_full_name = "astropy.coordinates.SkyOffsetFrame"
354	    elif (
355	        cls_full_name not in __construct_mixin_classes
356	        and not cls_full_name.startswith("astropy.coordinates.builtin_frames")
357	    ):
358	        raise ValueError(f"unsupported class for construct {cls_full_name}")
359	
360	    mod_name, _, cls_name = cls_full_name.rpartition(".")
361	    module = import_module(mod_name)
362	    cls = getattr(module, cls_name)
363	    for attr, value in info.items():
364	        if attr in cls.info.attrs_from_parent:
365	            obj_attrs[attr] = value
366	    mixin = cls.info._construct_from_dict(obj_attrs)
367	    for attr, value in info.items():
368	        if attr not in obj_attrs:
369	            setattr(mixin.info, attr, value)
370	    return mixin

373	class _TableLite(OrderedDict):
374	    """
375	    Minimal table-like object for _construct_mixin_from_columns.  This allows
376	    manipulating the object like a Table but without the actual overhead
377	    for a full Table.
378	
379	    More pressing, there is an issue with constructing MaskedColumn, where the
380	    encoded Column components (data, mask) are turned into a MaskedColumn.
381	    When this happens in a real table then all other columns are immediately
382	    Masked and a warning is issued. This is not desirable.
383	    """
384	
385	    def add_column(self, col, index=0):
386	        colnames = self.colnames
387	        self[col.info.name] = col
388	        for ii, name in enumerate(colnames):
389	            if ii >= index:
390	                self.move_to_end(name)
391	
392	    @property
393	    def colnames(self):
394	        return list(self.keys())
395	
396	    def itercols(self):
397	        return self.values()

400	def _construct_mixin_from_columns(new_name, obj_attrs, out):
401	    data_attrs_map = {}
402	    for name, val in obj_attrs.items():
403	        if isinstance(val, SerializedColumn):
404	            # A SerializedColumn can just link to a serialized column using a name
405	            # (e.g., time.jd1), or itself be a mixin (e.g., coord.obstime).  Note
406	            # that in principle a mixin could have include a column called 'name',
407	            # hence we check whether the value is actually a string (see gh-13232).
408	            if "name" in val and isinstance(val["name"], str):
409	                data_attrs_map[val["name"]] = name
410	            else:
411	                out_name = f"{new_name}.{name}"
412	                _construct_mixin_from_columns(out_name, val, out)
413	                data_attrs_map[out_name] = name
414	
415	    for name in data_attrs_map.values():
416	        del obj_attrs[name]
417	
418	    # The order of data_attrs_map may not match the actual order, as it is set
419	    # by the yaml description.  So, sort names by position in the serialized table.
420	    # Keep the index of the first column, so we can insert the new one there later.
421	    names = sorted(data_attrs_map, key=out.colnames.index)
422	    idx = out.colnames.index(names[0])
423	
424	    # Name is the column name in the table (e.g. "coord.ra") and
425	    # data_attr is the object attribute name  (e.g. "ra").  A different
426	    # example would be a formatted time object that would have (e.g.)
427	    # "time_col" and "value", respectively.
428	    for name in names:
429	        obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]] = out[name]
430	        del out[name]
431	
432	    info = obj_attrs.pop("__info__", {})
433	    if len(names) == 1:
434	        # col is the first and only serialized column; in that case, use info
435	        # stored on the column. First step is to get that first column which
436	        # has been moved from `out` to `obj_attrs` above.
437	        col = obj_attrs[data_attrs_map[name]]
438	
439	        # Now copy the relevant attributes
440	        for attr, nontrivial in (
441	            ("unit", lambda x: x not in (None, "")),
442	            ("format", lambda x: x is not None),
443	            ("description", lambda x: x is not None),
444	            ("meta", lambda x: x),
445	        ):
446	            col_attr = getattr(col.info, attr)
447	            if nontrivial(col_attr):
448	                info[attr] = col_attr
449	
450	    info["name"] = new_name
451	    col = _construct_mixin_from_obj_attrs_and_info(obj_attrs, info)
452	    out.add_column(col, index=idx)
453	
454	
455	def _construct_mixins_from_columns(tbl):
456	    if "__serialized_columns__" not in tbl.meta:
457	        return tbl
458	
459	    meta = tbl.meta.copy()
460	    mixin_cols = meta.pop("__serialized_columns__")
461	
462	    out = _TableLite(tbl.columns)
463	
464	    for new_name, obj_attrs in mixin_cols.items():
465	        _construct_mixin_from_columns(new_name, obj_attrs, out)
466	
467	    # If no quantity subclasses are in the output then output as Table.
468	    # For instance ascii.read(file, format='ecsv') doesn't specify an
469	    # output class and should return the minimal table class that
470	    # represents the table file.
471	    has_quantities = any(isinstance(col.info, QuantityInfo) for col in out.itercols())
472	    out_cls = QTable if has_quantities else Table
473	
474	    return out_cls(list(out.values()), names=out.colnames, copy=False, meta=meta)

```python
def col_copy(col, copy_indices=True):
    """
    Mixin-safe version of Column.copy() (with copy_data=True).

    Parameters
    ----------
    col : Column or mixin column
        Input column
    copy_indices : bool
        Copy the column ``indices`` attribute

    Returns
    -------
    col : Copy of input column
    """
    if isinstance(col, BaseColumn):
        return col.copy()

    newcol = col.copy() if hasattr(col, "copy") else deepcopy(col)
    # If the column has info defined, we copy it and adjust any indices
    # to point to the copied column.  By guarding with the if statement,
    # we avoid side effects (of creating the default info instance).
    if "info" in col.__dict__:
        newcol.info = col.info
        if copy_indices and col.info.indices:
            newcol.info.indices = deepcopy(col.info.indices)
            for index in newcol.info.indices:
                index.replace_col(col, newcol)

    return newcol
```

The conditional check `not isinstance(col, BaseColumn)` (inverted as `isinstance(col, BaseColumn)`) is intentional:

1. **For BaseColumn instances** (Column, MaskedColumn - the base column class): Use the optimized `col.copy()` method which is provided by the BaseColumn class itself.
